His work represents a profound [stylistic](/wiki/Painting_style "Painting style") shift from the calm [perspectival](/wiki/Perspective_(graphical) "Perspective (graphical)") regularity that characterized the art of the [Florentine Renaissance](/wiki/Italian_Renaissance "Italian Renaissance").[[1]](#cite_note-1) He is famous for his use of twining poses, coupled with ambiguous perspective; his figures often seem to float in an uncertain environment, unhampered by the forces of gravity.

Florentine Mannerist painter

| Pontormo | |

| Died | January 2, 1557(1557-01-02) (aged 62)  [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence"), Republic of Florence |
| Known for | Painting |

**Jacopo Carucci** or **Carrucci** (IPA: [[ˈjaːkopo ka(r)ˈruttʃi]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian"); May 24, 1494 – January 2, 1557), usually known as **Jacopo** (**da**) **Pontormo** or simply **Pontormo** (IPA: [[ponˈtormo]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian")), was an Italian [Mannerist](/wiki/Mannerism "Mannerism") painter and portraitist from the [Florentine School](/wiki/Florentine_School "Florentine School"). His work represents a profound [stylistic](/wiki/Painting_style "Painting style") shift from the calm [perspectival](/wiki/Perspective_(graphical) "Perspective (graphical)") regularity that characterized the art of the [Florentine Renaissance](/wiki/Italian_Renaissance "Italian Renaissance").[[1]](#cite_note-1) He is famous for his use of twining poses, coupled with ambiguous perspective; his figures often seem to float in an uncertain environment, unhampered by the forces of gravity.

## Biography and early work

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pontormo&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biography and early work")]

Jacopo Carucci was born at Pontorme (then known as Pontormo or Puntormo), near [Empoli](/wiki/Empoli "Empoli"), to Bartolomeo di Jacopo di Martino Carrucci and Alessandra di Pasquale di Zanobi. [Vasari](/wiki/Giorgio_Vasari "Giorgio Vasari") relates how the orphaned boy, "young, melancholy, and lonely", was shuttled around as a young apprentice:

> Jacopo had not been many months in Florence before Bernardo Vettori sent him to stay with [Leonardo da Vinci](/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci "Leonardo da Vinci"), and then with [Mariotto Albertinelli](/wiki/Mariotto_Albertinelli "Mariotto Albertinelli"), [Piero di Cosimo](/wiki/Piero_di_Cosimo "Piero di Cosimo"), and finally, in 1512, with [Andrea del Sarto](/wiki/Andrea_del_Sarto "Andrea del Sarto"), with whom he did not remain long, for after he had done the cartoons for the arch of the Servites, it does not seem that Andrea bore him any good will, whatever the cause may have been.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Pontormo%2C_Visitation%2C_1516%2C_SS_Annunziata%2C_Chiostrino_dei_Voti%2C_Florence.jpg/330px-Pontormo%2C_Visitation%2C_1516%2C_SS_Annunziata%2C_Chiostrino_dei_Voti%2C_Florence.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pontormo,_Visitation,_1516,_SS_Annunziata,_Chiostrino_dei_Voti,_Florence.jpg)

*Visitation*, 1514–16, [fresco](/wiki/Fresco "Fresco"), 392 × 337 cm, Chiostrino dei Voti, [SS. Annunziata, Florence](/wiki/SS._Annunziata,_Florence "SS. Annunziata, Florence")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Pontormo-visitation-after-restorationRGB.jpg/250px-Pontormo-visitation-after-restorationRGB.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pontormo-visitation-after-restorationRGB.jpg)

*[Visitation](/wiki/Carmignano_Visitation "Carmignano Visitation")*, 1528–29, [San Michele e San Francesco, Carmignano](/wiki/San_Michele_e_San_Francesco,_Carmignano "San Michele e San Francesco, Carmignano")

Pontormo painted in and around [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence"), often supported by [Medici](/wiki/House_of_Medici "House of Medici") patronage. A foray to Rome, largely to see [Michelangelo](/wiki/Michelangelo "Michelangelo")'s work, influenced his later style. Haunted faces and elongated bodies are characteristic of his work. An example of Pontormo's early style is a [fresco](/wiki/Fresco "Fresco") depicting the *[Visitation of the Virgin and St Elizabeth](/wiki/Visitation_of_the_Virgin_and_St_Elizabeth "Visitation of the Virgin and St Elizabeth"),* with its dancelike, balanced figures, painted from 1514 to 1516.

This early *Visitation* makes an interesting comparison with his painting of the same subject which was done about a decade later, now housed in the parish church of [St. Michael Archangel](/wiki/San_Michele_e_San_Francesco,_Carmignano "San Michele e San Francesco, Carmignano") in [Carmignano](/wiki/Carmignano "Carmignano"), about 20 km west of Florence. Placing these two pictures together—one from his early style, and another from his mature period—throws Pontormo's artistic development into sharp relief. In the earlier work, Pontormo is much closer in style to his teacher, Andrea del Sarto, and to the early sixteenth-century renaissance artistic principles. For example, the figures stand at just under half the height of the overall picture, and though a bit more crowded than true high renaissance balance would prefer, at least are placed in a [classicizing architectural](/wiki/Renaissance_architecture "Renaissance architecture") setting at a comfortable distance from the viewer. In the later work, the viewer is brought almost uncomfortably close to the [Virgin](/wiki/Mary_(mother_of_Jesus) "Mary (mother of Jesus)") and [St. Elizabeth](/wiki/Elizabeth_(biblical_figure) "Elizabeth (biblical figure)"), who drift toward each other in clouds of [drapery](/wiki/Drapery "Drapery"). Moreover, the clear architectural setting that is carefully constructed in the earlier piece has been completely abandoned in favour of a peculiar nondescript urban setting.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Jacopo_Pontormo_032.jpg/500px-Jacopo_Pontormo_032.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jacopo_Pontormo_032.jpg)

*[Joseph in Egypt](/wiki/Joseph_in_Egypt_(painting) "Joseph in Egypt (painting)")*, 1515–1518; Oil on wood; 96 x 109 cm; National Gallery, London

The [Joseph](/wiki/Joseph_(son_of_Jacob) "Joseph (son of Jacob)") canvases (now in the [National Gallery](/wiki/National_Gallery "National Gallery") in London) offer another example of Pontormo's developing style. Done around the same time as the earlier *Visitation*, these works (such as *[Joseph in Egypt](/wiki/Joseph_in_Egypt_(painting) "Joseph in Egypt (painting)")*, at left) show a much more mannerist leaning. According to [Giorgio Vasari](/wiki/Giorgio_Vasari "Giorgio Vasari"), the sitter for the boy seated on a step is his young apprentice, [Bronzino](/wiki/Bronzino "Bronzino").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Pontormo%2C_lunetta_di_vertumno_e_pomona%2C_1519-21%2C_03-93.jpg/330px-Pontormo%2C_lunetta_di_vertumno_e_pomona%2C_1519-21%2C_03-93.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pontormo,_lunetta_di_vertumno_e_pomona,_1519-21,_03-93.jpg)

Lunette of *[Vertumnus and Pomona](/wiki/Vertumnus_and_Pomona_(Pontormo) "Vertumnus and Pomona (Pontormo)")*, 1520–21

In the years between the SS Annunziata and San Michele *Visitation*s, Pontormo took part in the [fresco](/wiki/Fresco "Fresco") decoration of the salon of the Medici country [villa](/wiki/Villa "Villa") at [Poggio a Caiano](/wiki/Poggio_a_Caiano "Poggio a Caiano") (1519–20), 17 km NNW of Florence. There he painted frescoes in a [pastoral](/wiki/Pastoral "Pastoral") genre style, very uncommon for Florentine painters; their subject was the obscure [classical myth](/wiki/Classical_myth "Classical myth") of *[Vertumnus and Pomona](/wiki/Vertumnus_and_Pomona_(Pontormo) "Vertumnus and Pomona (Pontormo)")* in a [lunette](/wiki/Lunette "Lunette").

In 1522, when the [plague](/wiki/Plague_(disease) "Plague (disease)") broke out in Florence, Pontormo left for the [Certosa di Galluzzo](/wiki/Certosa_di_Galluzzo "Certosa di Galluzzo"), a cloistered [Carthusian monastery](/wiki/Carthusian_monastery "Carthusian monastery") where the [monks](/wiki/Monk "Monk") followed vows of silence. He painted a series of frescoes, now quite damaged, on the passion and resurrection of Christ. These frescoes reveal especially strongly the influence of [Albrecht Dürer](/wiki/Albrecht_D%C3%BCrer "Albrecht Dürer")'s engravings, which often provided inspiration to Pontormo after he returned to Florence.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Main works in Florence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pontormo&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Main works in Florence")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Jacopo_Pontormo_-_Kreuzabnahme_Christi.jpg/250px-Jacopo_Pontormo_-_Kreuzabnahme_Christi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jacopo_Pontormo_-_Kreuzabnahme_Christi.jpg)

*The Deposition from the Cross*, 1525–1528

The large altarpiece canvas for the [Brunelleschi](/wiki/Filippo_Brunelleschi "Filippo Brunelleschi")-designed [Capponi Chapel](/wiki/Capponi_Chapel "Capponi Chapel") in the church of [Santa Felicita, Florence](/wiki/Santa_Felicita,_Florence "Santa Felicita, Florence"), portraying *[The Deposition from the Cross](/wiki/The_Deposition_from_the_Cross_(Pontormo) "The Deposition from the Cross (Pontormo)")* (1528), is considered by many Pontormo's surviving masterpiece.

The figures, with their sharply modelled forms and brilliant colours, are united in an enormously complex, swirling ovular [composition](/wiki/Composition_(visual_arts) "Composition (visual arts)"), housed by a shallow, somewhat flattened space. Although commonly known as *The Deposition from the Cross*, there is no actual [cross](/wiki/Christian_cross "Christian cross") in the picture. The scene might more properly be called a *[Lamentation](/wiki/Lamentation_of_Christ "Lamentation of Christ")* or *Bearing the Body of Christ*. Those who are lowering (or supporting) [Christ](/wiki/Christ "Christ") appear as anguished as the mourners. Though they are bearing the weight of a full-grown man, they barely seem to be touching the ground; the lower figure in particular balances delicately and implausibly on his front two toes. These two boys have sometimes been interpreted as [angels](/wiki/Angel "Angel"), carrying Christ in his journey to [Heaven](/wiki/Heaven "Heaven"). In this case, the subject of the picture would be more akin to an *[Entombment](/wiki/Entombment "Entombment")*, though the lack of any discernible [tomb](/wiki/Tomb "Tomb") disrupts that theory, just as the lack of cross poses a problem for the *Deposition* interpretation. Finally, it has also been noted that the positions of Christ and the Virgin seem to echo those of [Michelangelo's *Pietà*](/wiki/Piet%C3%A0_(Michelangelo) "Pietà (Michelangelo)") in Rome, though here in the *Deposition* mother and son have been separated. Thus in addition to elements of a *Lamentation* and *Entombment*, this picture carries hints of a *[Pietà](/wiki/Piet%C3%A0 "Pietà")*.[[3]](#cite_note-3) It has been speculated that the bearded figure in the background at the far right is a [self-portrait](/wiki/Self-portrait "Self-portrait") of Pontormo as [Joseph of Arimathea](/wiki/Joseph_of_Arimathea "Joseph of Arimathea"). Another unique feature of this particular [*Deposition*](/wiki/Stations_of_the_Cross "Stations of the Cross") is the empty space occupying the central pictorial plane as all the Biblical personages seem to fall back from this point. It has been suggested that this emptiness may be a physical representation of the [Virgin Mary](/wiki/Virgin_Mary "Virgin Mary")'s emotional emptiness at the prospect of losing her son.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Pontormoannunciation.jpg/250px-Pontormoannunciation.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pontormoannunciation.jpg)

The *[Annunciation](/wiki/Annunciation_(Pontormo) "Annunciation (Pontormo)")*, fresco

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Pontormo%2C_Supper_at_Emmaus%2C_1525%2C_Uffizi.jpg/250px-Pontormo%2C_Supper_at_Emmaus%2C_1525%2C_Uffizi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pontormo,_Supper_at_Emmaus,_1525,_Uffizi.jpg)

*Supper at Emmaus,* 1525, Uffizi, Florence

On the wall to the right of the *Deposition*, Pontormo frescoed an *[Annunciation](/wiki/Annunciation_(Pontormo) "Annunciation (Pontormo)")* scene (at left). As with the *Deposition*, the artist's primary attention is on the figures themselves rather than their setting. Placed against white walls, the Angel [Gabriel](/wiki/Gabriel "Gabriel") and [Virgin Mary](/wiki/Mary_(mother_of_Jesus) "Mary (mother of Jesus)") are presented in an environment that is so simplified as to almost seem stark. The fictive architectural details above each of them, are painted to resemble the gray stone *pietra serena* that adorns the interior of Santa Felicità, thus uniting their painted space with the viewer's actual space. The startling contrast between the figures and ground makes their brilliant garments almost seem to glow in the light of the window between them, against the stripped-down background, as if the couple miraculously appeared in an extension of the chapel wall. The *Annunciation* resembles his above-mentioned *Visitation* in the church of San Michele at [Carmignano](/wiki/Carmignano "Carmignano") in both the style and swaying postures.

Vasari tells us that the [cupola](/wiki/Cupola "Cupola") was originally painted with [God the Father](/wiki/God_the_Father "God the Father") and [Four Patriarchs](/wiki/Patriarchs_(Bible) "Patriarchs (Bible)"). The decoration in the [dome](/wiki/Dome "Dome") of the chapel is now lost, but four [roundels](/wiki/Roundel "Roundel") with the [Evangelists](/wiki/Four_Evangelists "Four Evangelists") still adorn the [pendentives](/wiki/Pendentive "Pendentive"), worked on by both Pontormo and his chief pupil [Agnolo Bronzino](/wiki/Agnolo_Bronzino "Agnolo Bronzino"). The two artists collaborated so closely that specialists dispute which roundels each of them painted.

This tumultuous oval of figures took three years for Pontormo to complete. According to Vasari, because Pontormo desired above all to "do things his own way without being bothered by anyone," the artist screened off the chapel so as to prevent interfering opinions. Vasari continues, "And so, having painted it in his own way without any of his friends being able to point anything out to him, it was finally uncovered and seen with astonishment by all of Florence..."[[4]](#cite_note-4)

A number of Pontormo's other works have also remained in Florence; the [Uffizi Gallery](/wiki/Uffizi_Gallery "Uffizi Gallery") holds his mystical *[Supper at Emmaus](/wiki/Supper_at_Emmaus_(Pontormo) "Supper at Emmaus (Pontormo)")* as well as portraits.

Many of Pontormo's well-known canvases, such as the early *Joseph in Egypt* series (c. 1515) and the later *Martyrdom of St Maurice and the Theban Legion* (c. 1531) depict crowds milling about in extreme [contrapposto](/wiki/Contrapposto "Contrapposto") of greatly varied positions.

His portraits, acutely characterized, show similarly Mannerist proportions.

## Lost or damaged works

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pontormo&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Lost or damaged works")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/Jacopo_Pontormo_-_Christ_the_Judge_with_the_Creation_of_Eve_-_WGA18134.jpg/250px-Jacopo_Pontormo_-_Christ_the_Judge_with_the_Creation_of_Eve_-_WGA18134.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jacopo_Pontormo_-_Christ_the_Judge_with_the_Creation_of_Eve_-_WGA18134.jpg)

*Christ and Creation of Eve*

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/Jacopo_Pontormo_-_Study_for_Deluge_%28portion_of_sheet%29_-_WGA18142.jpg/250px-Jacopo_Pontormo_-_Study_for_Deluge_%28portion_of_sheet%29_-_WGA18142.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jacopo_Pontormo_-_Study_for_Deluge_(portion_of_sheet)_-_WGA18142.jpg)

*Study for Deluge*

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Jacopo_Pontormo_-_Group_of_the_Dead_-_WGA18133.jpg/250px-Jacopo_Pontormo_-_Group_of_the_Dead_-_WGA18133.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jacopo_Pontormo_-_Group_of_the_Dead_-_WGA18133.jpg)

*Dead in Last Judgment*

Many of Pontormo's works have been damaged, including the lunettes for the cloister in the [Carthusian monastery of Galluzo](/wiki/Florence_Charterhouse "Florence Charterhouse"). They now are displayed indoors, although in their damaged state.

Perhaps most tragic is the loss of the unfinished frescoes for the choir of the [Basilica of San Lorenzo, Florence](/wiki/Basilica_of_San_Lorenzo,_Florence "Basilica of San Lorenzo, Florence") which consumed the last decade of his life.[[5]](#cite_note-5) His frescoes depicted a [Last Judgment](/wiki/Last_Judgment "Last Judgment") day composed of an unsettling morass of writhing figures. The remaining drawings, showing a bizarre and mystical ribboning of bodies, had an almost hallucinatory effect. Florentine figure painting had mainly stressed linear and sculptural figures. For example, the Christ in [Michelangelo's *Last Judgment*](/wiki/The_Last_Judgment_(Michelangelo) "The Last Judgment (Michelangelo)") in the [Sistine Chapel](/wiki/Sistine_Chapel "Sistine Chapel") is a massive painted block, stern in his wrath; by contrast, Pontormo's Jesus in the *Last Judgment* twists sinuously, as if rippling through the heavens in the dance of ultimate finality. Angels swirl about him in even more serpentine poses. If Pontormo's work from the 1520s seemed to float in a world little touched by gravitational force, the *Last Judgment* figures seem to have escaped it altogether and flail through rarefied air.

In his *Last Judgment,* Pontormo went against pictorial and theological tradition by placing God the Father at the feet of Christ, instead of above him, an idea [Vasari](/wiki/Vasari "Vasari") found deeply disturbing:

> But I have never been able to understand the significance of this scene, ... I mean, what he could have intended to signify in that part where there is Christ on high, raising the dead, and below His feet is God the Father, who is creating Adam and Eve. Besides this, in one of the corners, where are the four Evangelists, nude, with books in their hands, it does not seem to me that in a single place did he give a thought to any order of composition, or measurement, or time, or variety in the heads, or diversity in the flesh-colours, or, in a word, to any rule, proportion or law of perspective, for the whole work is full of nude figures with an order, design, invention, composition, colouring, and painting contrived after his own fashion ...

## Critical assessment and legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pontormo&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Critical assessment and legacy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Pontormo_%28Jacopo_Carucci%29_%28Italian%2C_Florentine%29_-_Portrait_of_a_Halberdier_%28Francesco_Guardi%3F%29_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/250px-Pontormo_%28Jacopo_Carucci%29_%28Italian%2C_Florentine%29_-_Portrait_of_a_Halberdier_%28Francesco_Guardi%3F%29_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pontormo_(Jacopo_Carucci)_(Italian,_Florentine)_-_Portrait_of_a_Halberdier_(Francesco_Guardi%3F)_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

*[Portrait of a Halberdier](/wiki/Portrait_of_a_Halberdier "Portrait of a Halberdier")*, 1528–1530, oil on canvas, J. Paul Getty Museum, Los Angeles

Vasari's *[Life](/wiki/Lives_of_the_Most_Excellent_Painters,_Sculptors,_and_Architects "Lives of the Most Excellent Painters, Sculptors, and Architects")* of Pontormo depicts him as withdrawn and steeped in neurosis while at the centre of the artists and patrons of his lifetime. This image of Pontormo has tended to colour the popular conception of the artist, as seen in the film of [Giovanni Fago](/wiki/Giovanni_Fago "Giovanni Fago"), *Pontormo, a heretical love*. Fago portrays Pontormo as mired in a lonely and ultimately paranoid dedication to his final *Last Judgment* project, which he often kept shielded from onlookers. Yet as the art historian Elizabeth Pilliod has pointed out, Vasari was in fierce competition with the Pontormo/Bronzino workshop at the time when he was writing his *[Lives of the Most Excellent Painters, Sculptors, and Architects](/wiki/Lives_of_the_Most_Excellent_Painters,_Sculptors,_and_Architects "Lives of the Most Excellent Painters, Sculptors, and Architects")*. This professional rivalry between the two *bottega*s could well have provided Vasari with ample motivation for running down the artistic lineage of his opponent for [Medici](/wiki/Medici "Medici") patronage.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Perhaps as a result of Vasari's derision, or perhaps because of the vagaries of aesthetic taste, Pontormo's work was quite out of fashion for several centuries. The fact that so much of his work has been lost or severely damaged is a testament to this neglect, though he has received renewed attention from contemporary art historians. Indeed, between 1989 and 2002, Pontormo's *[Portrait of a Halberdier](/wiki/Portrait_of_a_Halberdier "Portrait of a Halberdier")* (at right), held the title of the world's [most expensive painting](/wiki/List_of_most_expensive_paintings "List of most expensive paintings") by an [Old Master](/wiki/Old_Master "Old Master").

Regardless of the veracity of Vasari's account, it is certainly true that Pontormo's artistic idiosyncrasies produced a style that few were able (or willing) to imitate, with the exception of his closest pupil [Bronzino](/wiki/Bronzino "Bronzino"). Bronzino's early work is so close to that of his teacher, that the authorship of several paintings from the 1520s and '30s is still under dispute—for example, the four *tondi* containing the [Evangelists](/wiki/Four_Evangelists "Four Evangelists") in the Capponi Chapel.

Pontormo shares some of the mannerism of [Rosso Fiorentino](/wiki/Rosso_Fiorentino "Rosso Fiorentino") and of [Parmigianino](/wiki/Parmigianino "Parmigianino"). In some ways, he anticipated the [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque "Baroque") as well as the tensions of [El Greco](/wiki/El_Greco "El Greco"). His eccentricities also resulted in an original sense of composition. At best, his compositions are cohesive. The figures in the *Deposition*, for example, appear to sustain each other: removal of any one of them would cause the edifice to collapse. In other works, as in the Joseph canvases, the crowding makes for a confusing pictorial melee. It is in the later drawings that we see a graceful fusion of bodies in a composition which includes the oval frame of Jesus in the *Last Judgement*.
